_comment: Teacher-authored ideal hints (labels), keyed '<question_id>::<label>'. Rules: 1-2 sentences; no praise; never reveal complexity values on a suboptimal state; on a correct solution always ask time+space complexity; never quote test cases, expected outputs, or tracebacks (candidate can't see them); reference only the candidate's own code and the problem statement; if no code, just say so.
lc-1::stub: You haven't written any code yet.
lc-1::logic-bug: You're returning the two values that add up to the target. Re-read the prompt — what is it actually asking you to return?
lc-1::suboptimal-ok: It works, but it checks every pair. Could you find the match in a single pass — what would you remember as you go?
lc-1::runtime-error: Your code crashes before it returns anything. Look at the line where you store into `seen` — what are you using as the key, one value or the whole list?
lc-1::typo-var: Your code crashes before it returns. Look at the name you compare the sum against inside the `if` — is it spelled the same as the parameter?
lc-1::optimal-ok: That works. What are its time and space complexity? Then a follow-up: if the array were already sorted, could you solve it without the extra dictionary?
lc-20::stub: You haven't written any code yet.
lc-20::count-bug: You're only tallying opening versus closing brackets, so nothing enforces that they match in type and order. What would let you remember the most recent unclosed bracket to check against?
lc-20::typo-method: Your code crashes before it returns. Check the method you call on `stack` when you see an opening bracket — is that an actual list method?
lc-20::stack-ok: That handles the given cases. What are its time and space complexity? And a follow-up: how would you adapt it if the string could also contain a `*` that can stand for `(`, `)`, or nothing?
lc-70::stub: You haven't written any code yet.
lc-70::wrong: You're returning `n` itself, but the number of ways to climb isn't the number of steps. How many ways reach step n given the steps just below it?
lc-70::typo-var: Your code crashes before it returns. Look at the variable on your `return` line — is it one you actually defined?
lc-70::optimal-ok: That works. What are its time and space complexity? Follow-up: what if you could take 1, 2, or 3 steps at a time?
lc-121::stub: You haven't written any code yet.
lc-121::wrong-order: You're taking the overall max minus the overall min, but you can only sell on a day after you buy. How does that constraint change which pairs are allowed?
lc-121::suboptimal-ok: It works, but it compares every pair of days. Could you do it in a single scan — what is the one thing worth tracking as you move forward?
lc-121::runtime-error: Your code crashes before it returns. Look at the variable you reference inside the `max(...)` call — is it spelled the same as where you defined it?
lc-121::typo-syntax: Your code won't run — there's a syntax error. Look at the `for` line: is anything missing at the end of it?
lc-121::optimal-ok: That works. What are its time and space complexity? Then a follow-up: what if you were allowed at most two transactions instead of just one?
lc-141::stub: You haven't written any code yet.
lc-141::wrong: You always return `False`, so a list that does loop back would be missed. As you walk the list, what would tell you that you've reached a node you've already visited?
lc-141::typo-attr: Your code crashes before it returns. Check the attribute you use to advance `slow` — is it the same field name the nodes actually have?
lc-141::optimal-ok: That works. What are its time and space complexity? Could you do it without any extra data structure?
lc-206::stub: You haven't written any code yet.
lc-206::wrong: You're returning the list as-is without changing any links. To reverse it, what does each node's `next` need to point to instead?
lc-206::typo-attr: Your code crashes before it returns. Check the field you read into `nxt` — do the nodes actually have an attribute by that name?
lc-206::optimal-ok: That works. What are its time and space complexity? Could you also do it recursively?
lc-226::stub: You haven't written any code yet.
lc-226::wrong: You're returning the tree unchanged. What has to happen to each node's two children for the tree to come out mirrored?
lc-226::typo-attr: Your code crashes before it returns. Look at the child attribute you pass into the recursive call — is it spelled the same as the node's actual field?
lc-226::optimal-ok: That works. What are its time and space complexity? Follow-up: could you do it iteratively with a queue?
lc-242::stub: You haven't written any code yet.
lc-242::wrong: You're only comparing the two lengths, but equal length doesn't mean equal letters. What would you compare to know they use exactly the same characters?
lc-242::typo-name: Your code crashes before it returns. Check the function name you call on `t` — is it spelled the same as the one you call on `s`?
lc-242::optimal-ok: That works. What are its time and space complexity? What does the extra space depend on?
lc-704::stub: You haven't written any code yet.
lc-704::wrong: You're returning whether the target is present, but the problem asks for its position. What do you return when you find it, and what when you don't?
lc-704::typo-var: Your code crashes before it returns. Look at how you update `lo` — is that variable spelled the same as where you defined it?
lc-704::optimal-ok: That works. What are its time and space complexity? Follow-up: what if the array were rotated?
lc-733::stub: You haven't written any code yet.
lc-733::wrong: You're only recoloring the starting pixel, not the region connected to it. From that pixel, how would you spread to the neighbors that share its original color?
lc-733::typo-var: Your code crashes before it returns. Check the value you assign into the pixel — is it spelled the same as the `color` parameter?
lc-733::optimal-ok: That works. What are its time and space complexity in terms of the image size? Why does the equal-color check matter?
lc-146::stub: You haven't written any code yet.
lc-146::wrong: Your cache stores every key but never removes anything, so it ignores the capacity limit. When you're at capacity and a new key arrives, which existing key should go?
lc-146::typo-method: Your code crashes on the first lookup. Check the method you call on `self.cache` in `get` — is that a real dict method?
lc-146::optimal-ok: That works. What's the time complexity of `get` and `put`? Could you achieve the same without OrderedDict?
lc-3::stub: You haven't written any code yet.
lc-3::wrong: You're counting distinct characters across the whole string, but the answer has to be a contiguous run with no repeats. How would you shrink your window when a character repeats?
lc-3::typo-var: Your code crashes before it returns. Look at the variable inside your `max(...)` window-length calc — is it spelled the same as where you set it?
lc-3::optimal-ok: That works. What are its time and space complexity? What's the most entries your map can hold?
lc-56::stub: You haven't written any code yet.
lc-56::wrong: You're returning the intervals untouched. What do you need to do first so that overlapping ones end up next to each other?
lc-56::typo-var: Your code crashes before it returns. Look at the value you append for a non-overlapping interval — is that variable defined?
lc-56::optimal-ok: That works. What are its time and space complexity? What dominates the running time?
lc-200::stub: You haven't written any code yet.
lc-200::wrong: You're counting every land cell, but cells that touch each other form one island. Once you find land, how would you mark the rest of that island so you don't count it again?
lc-200::typo-var: Your code crashes before it returns. Check the name you pass to `len(...)` on that line — is it the same as the grid parameter?
lc-200::optimal-ok: That works. What are its time and space complexity in terms of the grid size?
lc-322::stub: You haven't written any code yet.
lc-322::wrong: Taking the biggest coin first doesn't always give the fewest coins — a smaller coin can lead to a better total. How could you build up the best answer for every amount up to the target?

lc-322::optimal-ok: That works. What are its time and space complexity in terms of the amount and number of coins?
lc-102::stub: You haven't written any code yet.
lc-102::wrong: You're collecting all the values into one flat list, but the answer groups them by depth. How could you process the tree one level at a time?

lc-102::optimal-ok: That works. What are its time and space complexity? What decides the most nodes you hold in the queue at once?

lc-153::wrong: You're returning the first element, but rotation means the smallest value can be anywhere. What comparison tells you which half the minimum sits in?

lc-238::stub: You haven't written any code yet.
lc-238::wrong: You've got the running product of everything to the left of each index, but nothing from the right. What second pass would fold in the elements after each position?

lc-238::optimal-ok: That works. What are its time and space complexity? Does the output array count toward the extra space?
lc-739::stub: You haven't written any code yet.
lc-739::wrong: You're returning all zeros, which only holds for days with nothing warmer ahead. As you scan, how could you keep track of earlier days still waiting for a warmer one?
lc-739::typo-var: Your code crashes before it returns. Look at the variable on your `return` line — is it spelled the same as the list you built?
lc-739::optimal-ok: That works. What are its time and space complexity? How many times does each index enter and leave the stack?

lc-79::wrong: You're only checking that the first letter appears somewhere; you're not walking the rest of the word through adjacent cells. From a matching cell, how would you try to extend the match?

lc-79::optimal-ok: That works. What are its time and space complexity? How deep can the recursion go in the worst case?
lc-98::stub: You haven't written any code yet.
lc-98::wrong: You're accepting every tree as valid. A BST needs every node in the left subtree below the root and every node on the right above it — how would you carry those bounds down as you recurse?
lc-98::typo-attr: Your code crashes before it returns. Check the child attribute in your recursive call — is it spelled the same as the node's actual right field?
lc-98::optimal-ok: That works. What are its time and space complexity? What does the extra space depend on?

lc-417::wrong: You're returning an empty list, so you're not exploring where water can flow at all. From which cells is it easiest to start — the ocean edges or the interior?

lc-417::optimal-ok: That works. What are its time and space complexity in terms of the grid dimensions?
lc-435::stub: You haven't written any code yet.
lc-435::wrong: You're always returning 0, but some of these intervals really do overlap and must be removed. If you sort them, which one should you keep when two overlap to leave the most room?

lc-435::optimal-ok: That works. What are its time and space complexity? What dominates the runtime?
lc-128::stub: You haven't written any code yet.
lc-128::wrong: You're counting how many distinct numbers there are, but they have to form a run of consecutive values. Given a number, how would you check whether it starts a run and how long that run gets?
lc-128::typo-var: Your code crashes before it returns. Look at the variable inside your `max(...)` — is it spelled the same as the one you increment?
lc-128::optimal-ok: That works. What are its time and space complexity? Why is it enough to only start counting from numbers that have no predecessor in the set?
lc-49::stub: You haven't written any code yet.
lc-49::wrong: You're putting every word in its own group, so anagrams never get combined. What canonical form could two anagrams share that you could group by?
lc-49::typo-var: Your code crashes before it returns. Check the variable you append into the group — is it spelled the same as your loop variable?
lc-49::optimal-ok: That works. What are its time and space complexity in terms of the number of words and their length?
lc-297::stub: You haven't written any code yet.
lc-297::wrong: Your format records the values but not where the empty spots are, so you can't tell a left child from a right child when rebuilding. What marker could you emit for a missing child?
lc-297::typo-method: Your code crashes while rebuilding. Check the method you call on `data` to split it — is that spelled like a real string method?
lc-297::optimal-ok: That works. What are the time and space complexity of serialize and deserialize? What does your format's size depend on?
lc-239::stub: You haven't written any code yet.
lc-239::wrong: You're taking a max for every start index, including windows that run off the end and are shorter than k. How many valid windows of size k are there, and where should the last one start?
lc-239::typo-var: Your code crashes before it returns. Look at the variable on your `return` line — is it spelled the same as the list you appended to?
lc-239::optimal-ok: That works. What are its time and space complexity? How many times does each index enter and leave the deque?
lc-23::stub: You haven't written any code yet.
lc-23::wrong: You're returning just the first list, ignoring the rest. How could you combine the values from all the lists so the result stays sorted?
lc-23::typo-attr: Your code crashes before it returns. Check the field you use to advance `node` — is it the same attribute name the nodes have?
lc-23::optimal-ok: That works. What are its time and space complexity? Could you merge them pairwise with a heap instead of collecting all values?
lc-42::stub: You haven't written any code yet.
lc-42::wrong: You're returning 0, so you're not accounting for any trapped water. Above each bar, the water level is set by the tallest bars to its left and right — how could you track those as you scan inward?
lc-42::typo-var: Your code crashes before it returns. Look at the array name you index in the `else` branch — is it spelled the same as the `height` parameter?
lc-42::optimal-ok: That works. What are its time and space complexity? Can you justify why the two pointers never miss water?
lc-76::stub: You haven't written any code yet.
lc-76::wrong: You're returning the whole string, but the answer is the shortest window that still contains every needed character. How would you shrink the window from the left once it's valid?
lc-76::typo-var: Your code crashes before it returns. Look at the index you use inside the shrink step — is it spelled the same as your left pointer?
lc-76::optimal-ok: That works. What are its time and space complexity? How many times does each character get visited?
lc-3::suboptimal-ok: It works, but for each starting index you re-scan the rest of the string. Could a sliding window avoid re-examining characters you've already passed?
lc-704::suboptimal-ok: It works, but you're checking every element one by one. The array is sorted — how could you rule out half of it at each step?
lc-242::suboptimal-ok: It works, but sorting both strings does more than you need. Could you decide it in one pass by tallying characters?
lc-128::suboptimal-ok: It works, but sorting is doing the heavy lifting. Could you find the runs using a set and skip sorting entirely?
lc-238::suboptimal-ok: It works, but you recompute a full product for every index. Could you reuse running products from the left and the right instead?
lc-70::suboptimal-ok: It works, but it recomputes the same values many times over. What could you keep so each step is computed only once?
lc-739::suboptimal-ok: It works, but for each day you scan forward until you find a warmer one. Could a stack of days still waiting for a warmer one save that re-scanning?
lc-153::suboptimal-ok: It works, but it looks at every element. The array is sorted then rotated — how could you halve the search at each step?
lc-206::suboptimal-ok: It works, but you're using an extra list the size of the input. Could you reverse the links in place instead?
lc-42::suboptimal-ok: It works, but for each bar you re-scan left and right for the tallest. Could precomputing those maxes, or two pointers, avoid the rescanning?
lc-239::suboptimal-ok: It works, but you recompute a max over k elements for every window. Could a deque keep the running max without rescanning?
